era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-5th century BCE, during the period of Athenian expansion and consolidation of the Delian League. Cimon is at the height of his power and influence in Athens. Sparta remains the dominant land power in the Peloponnese. The Persian Empire under Artaxerxes I is consolidating its rule after the succession crisis following Xerxes' death. This period represents the early Classical age, characterized by the rise of democratic institutions in Athens and the development of Classical Greek culture.

events.title: Possible dating of the Battle of Eurymedon
events.description: Some scholars place the Battle of Eurymedon, a major Athenian victory against Persian forces, in 469 BCE rather than in the 460s BCE. Cimon led an Athenian naval and land force to victory against a combined Persian fleet and army. This battle is considered one of the most significant Athenian victories against the Persians and marked the effective end of major Persian naval operations in the eastern Mediterranean. The victory greatly enhanced Athenian prestige and power.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book I

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Eurymedon is well-attested in ancient sources, but its dating is highly uncertain. Modern scholars have proposed dates ranging from 469 to 465 BCE. The date of 469 BCE is one of several plausible reconstructions, favored by some scholars who use different chronological systems.
cross_references: Part of broader Athenian expansion against Persia

events.title: Birth of Socrates
events.description: According to traditional dating, the philosopher Socrates was born in Athens in 469 BCE. Socrates would become one of the most influential figures in Western philosophy, though he left no written works. His teachings, preserved through the writings of his student Plato and others, fundamentally shaped Greek and Western philosophy. However, the exact date of Socrates' birth is uncertain, and some sources place it in 470 or 471 BCE.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of the Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The traditional date of Socrates' birth is 469 BCE, but this is based on later sources and chronological reconstruction. Ancient sources do not provide a precise birth date. Modern scholars generally accept 469 BCE as a conventional date, but the actual year may have been 470 or 471 BCE.
cross_references: Related to the development of Classical Greek philosophy
Led to the philosophical tradition that shaped Western thought

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Eurymedon to 469 BCE is disputed. While some scholars favor this date, others argue for 466, 465, or even 464 BCE. The ancient sources themselves do not provide a precise date, and the reconstruction depends on uncertain chronological systems. The birth date of Socrates is also uncertain; while 469 BCE is the traditional date, some sources suggest 470 or 471 BCE. The uncertainty reflects the difficulty in precisely dating events from ancient sources that were written long after the events occurred.
historiographic_note: The year 469 BCE is poorly documented in surviving sources. The two events listed here—the possible Battle of Eurymedon and the birth of Socrates—are both subject to significant chronological uncertainty. The historical record for this year is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Thucydides, Plutarch, and later compilations. There is virtually no documentation from Persian, Egyptian, or other non-Greek perspectives. The dating of events in this period is highly uncertain and depends on the reconstruction of the Athenian archon list and other chronological systems that are themselves uncertain. The Western bias toward Greek history is pronounced, and the absence of documentation from other regions means that major events elsewhere are completely unrecorded in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Eurymedon may have occurred in 469, 467, 466, or 465 BCE; the dating is highly uncertain
